replies.content: I wish I did! replies.title: Re: DPRK Numerology
replies.content:
To my limited knowledge, traditional Korean numerology links very much to traditional Chinese numerology via the Yijing (I Ching), which as a Confucian classic was very influential in Korea. Of course, Chinese doesn't use an alphabet, so computational numerology of Chinese characters is not like computational numerology for alphabetic languages. Instead, characters are often decomposed into components that are more or less similar to numbers. (For example, the "three dot" water radical is interpreted as the number 3.)

I don't know whether Hangul, the Korean phonetic script, ever had a numerical interpretation, but I wouldn't be overwhelmed by astonishment to find out that it had at least one --especially by now.

LeGrand

replies.content: Hmm! Thank you for this.

replies.title: Re: A turning point.

I'm a bit late to the party on this, but I did some quick research on the matter from a numerological perspective and found some interesting things (please note I am a total amateur and I could very well be talking out of my rear end).

The first thing to consider is that the attempt on Trump happened nearly 112 years after the attempt on Theodore Roosevelt. The number 112 could hold some significance: the number 1 is repeated twice, representing strong individuality, the beginning of something new, assertiveness, and a good deal of thick-headedness and stubbornness that comes with it. The number 2 could be seen as a number of unity, related to the social, to the familiar; it's reminiscent to how Republicans seemed to have all united under Trump after the attempt, despite several party members and voters being somewhat outspoken in their dislike of him. Combine the number 112 with the image of Trump raising his fist behind the US flag (more on that in a bit), and this can be seen as not only a reaffirmation of his power as a headstrong individual, but also of his ability to unite people under him, which he most certainly did after this Saturday. Also, the root number of 112, after reducing it, is 4, which could represent balance, sturdiness and consistency, like the four sides of a square, or the four legs of a table keeping it firmly on the ground, and so on. No matter how much you try to push the orange man and no matter how stubborn and "Americanistic" he is, you can't keep him down.

Second, the date of the attempt was July 13th, 2024. That's 13/07/2024. Applying some extremely basic addition to reduce this date to its root number, we get, lo and behold: the number 1. I feel as if Pythagoras would get a chuckle out of this. One doesn't need to be particularly proficient in numerology to find there is some significance here. Of course, the number 13 is also reminiscent of the Death arcana, representing change, the end of an era, the replacement of the old with the new, a turning point and so on. Finally, the root number of 13 is 4. Refer to the above paragraph to see what I think about this number...

And third and lastly, this might not be related to numbers, but if one were to look closely at the iconic photo Guillem mentioned, one notices a peculiar thing: the US flag is upside down (tipped slightly diagonally, yes, but upside down). I've done some quick research on this as well and have found that in the United States, displaying American flag upside down can be seen as a sign of protest or mourning, of disagreeing with the direction the country is taking and believing that dark times lie ahead. It could be the image is less representative of Trump as a triumphant, all-American figure, and more like the last, persistent hurrah of a once great nation on its twilight years, if I may be so melodramatic...

Just some food for thought. Appreciate any opinions or corrections!

comments.title: Lévi, Ritual, chapter 7
comments.content:
Hello, Mr Greer,

I’m slowly reading through Lévi’s High Magic; and got to chapter 7 of Ritual, where he lists the seven ‘magical works.’ The list surprised me, because the works of a magician seemed, though they might be accurate referencing the medieval books, seemed opposed to my own views of what constitutes High Magic.

You and I agree that High Magic consists of Self-Transformation. Lévi’s first section on Theory seems to generally confirm my definition of High Magic. But I personally don’t see myself performing much of what Lévi describes in his second section on Ritual.

Is it me? Am I too idealistic? Or does Lévi get to the intricacies of High Magic later on towards the end of the book?

replies.title: Re: Lévi, Ritual, chapter 7
replies.content:
Keep in mind that Lévi was at the very dawn of the modern magical revival. He was trying to sketch out a framework for reviving a nearly dead tradition. As you'd expect, he had a good grasp of the theory but much more tentative ideas about the practice. It took another century or so of hard work to get the latter more or less clarified.

That said, remember that Lévi was deep into symbolism, and often wrote symbolically when he seemed to be writing in the most literal terms. He was good at that, as good as some of the old alchemists! Think about those seven magical works. What might they mean, if you looked at them in terms of the material in the fist half of the book?

replies.content: The kind of journaling I recommend for this sort of thing is very straightforward. Sit down with a pen and a notebook. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not JMG, and not occult, but as a diabetic:

2) if the anxiety is happening consistently at around the same time in the wee hours of the morning, like say around 3-4am, the first thing I'd do is start checking my blood sugar-- before bed, as soon as I wake up in the morning or any time at night. There are a lot of reasons that could happen, but one of the possibilities is low (or dropping quickly, which gets the same result even if it doesn't drop to hypo levels) blood glucose levels overnight, which causes your body to dump cortisol and adrenaline into your bloodstream. This will raise your glucose, but it'll also trigger the classic anxiety/panic response, often but not always as early-morning nightmares or panic attacks.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Interesting, thanks for this! How do you check your blood sugar/remedy the problem?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
May depend on where you live, but in the US, you can purchase a cheap glucometer and some test strips for around $40, without any kind of prescription. Spring-loaded lancets usually available in the same shelf, also cheap. They come with instructions. They're fun toys to play with, for any number of casual non-medical, DIY science purposes. I would, of course, never make a medical recommendation, as I am not a doctor.

There are any number of interesting educational resources you could peruse for more info with a few keywords such as "hypoglycemia and anxiety" "dawn phenomenon" "nocturnal hypoglycemia", and I have found Jenny Ruhl's website a very handy plain-English information clearinghouse on all things related to blood sugar regulation, including the normal range for healthy people. The human metabolism can get up to some weird shenanigans during sleep! For people who experience early-morning lows that result in nightmares, anxiety, jitters, and premature wakefulness, I *think* the usual recommendation is to have a little snack before bed. But that could be old or inaccurate info, and it's probably best to do your own research. I'm just some weirdo on the internet after all ;)

replies.title: Re: Video posted 4 Mo Ago @11 Min Mark Describes Trump Assassination Attempt in Great Detail?
replies.content:
Time's only linear for our physical selves. We're not in very good communication with the non-physical parts, but we do sometimes pick up bits and pieces of things we can "see" with our non-physical senses, which are not anchored to time in the same way. I think we all do this. The tricky bit is figuring out which parts are meaningful, just like with our physical senses. Doesn't matter how accurately you dreamed about that shopping cart denting a car 3000 miles away and 2 years in the future, you know. You'll never know if it was real, and it had no significance. You're already deluged with irrelevant details in your physical, timebound existence.

So... now and then somebody's going to pick up a relevant detail. The problem is sorting it out from all the completely irrelevant ones.

replies.title: Re: Video posted 4 Mo Ago @11 Min Mark Describes Trump Assassination Attempt in Great Detail?
replies.content: Huh, now that's a really interesting insight - maybe one of the main differences between our physical and non-physical senses is that our physical senses have tighter, more consistent feedback loops that teach us what they mean (as well as what to ignore), whereas getting that with non-physical senses takes more practice for most of us. Watching small children learn to navigate their bodies and senses has given me a new appreciation for how much we have to learn about what seems "automatic" later on.

replies.title: Re: Video posted 4 Mo Ago @11 Min Mark Describes Trump Assassination Attempt in Great Detail?
replies.content:
Plus, with your physical senses, you grow up constantly having people verify, interpret, label, and reinforce your perceptions. Until you mostly see what you know is there, rather than what the light is bouncing off of. It's why, in order to draw well with good perspective... you have to un-train yourself to a large extent, so that you can see what's actually reaching your eyeballs *instead* of what you know/assume about it.

With your non-physical senses, you grow up being told "that's not real" or "you can't know that" until you either stop seeing it entirely, or you self-censor. Nobody gives you a handy roadmap on how to interpret any of it, the way they tell you "red, blue, green, tree, leaf, book, eye, nose, hand" from babyhood... so for everything else, you're very much on your own, and you're lucky if any of it ever makes proper sense.

Or at any rate, that's my working hypothesis. If you've got a better one, I'm listening.

replies.title: Re: Video posted 4 Mo Ago @11 Min Mark Describes Trump Assassination Attempt in Great Detail?

I was including reinforcement from those around you in the "feedback loops" I mentioned, and now that you bring up drawing, that's an excellent point - Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain is an excellent lesson in how much of "perception" is actually down to a state of consciousness.

Another point you raise with fascinating implications is the usefulness of a shared vocabulary in labeling what you perceive for solidifying your ability to perceive any given cluster of stimuli as a "thing." "This range of the EM spectrum is 'red,' that is 'blue'" or "The big brown and green complex thing is a tree, the little green bit is a leaf." I have to imagine a lack of handy labels like that might have something to do with the fuzziness of etheric/astral/mental perceptions (aside from any inherent ineffability, of course).

Thanks again for some great food for thought!
Jeff

Yes, Methylethyl, we do all do this, but you do it way, way more than average. You have opened a permanent pathway (at least for this lifetime) between your conscious awareness and the part of your soul that dwells in the dimension of fate. That's the same part of the soul that expresses as willpower, i.e. the ability to flow with or against the direction fate is moving in.

That pathway is tiny, so it's not as though you're flooded with access to all the contents of that part of your eternal soul — that would probably be a fatally overwhelming experience! However, you can catch all kinds of glimpses of fate that most people can't, as well as glimpses of how acts of will might affect fate. That's one of the oxymorons that determinists can't handle — that being located in the web of fate also means being located in the web of will, or, said another way, that adding up all the competing wills in the universe will leave you facing in the direction that fate is headed.

I love that you wrote, "We're not in very good communication with the non-physical parts," when you are in much better communication than most of us are! Seeing through time, catching glimpses of fate, and sensing how acts of will are ultimately going to affect fate, i.e. what we categorize as karma, are characteristic attributes of the saint's path. Historically, saints have had a deep understanding of how karma will accrete to various acts and choices. It's a bit of a lonely path, a bit miraculous, and, let's face it, somebody's gotta get the tracks established. Thank you, Methylethyl, for blazing the way for the rest of us.

— Christophe

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's important to keep in mind that the part of your subconscious screeching derangements is not you. It is a part of the greater ecosystem of energies that have accrued to you. Some part of its voice is an authentic communication and probable cry for help from your beleaguered soul, but your soul's goal is not its own self-destruction.

Others have taken their own destructive fixed energy patterns, which they couldn't figure out how to resolve, and imprinted them onto you, in the vain hope that that would offer them some reprieve. Just as someone once did to them... and someone else once did to them... and so on into the mists of time. Now that those fixed energy patterns have lodged as parasites in the larger ecosystem of your Self, you have the opportunity to break that abusive cycle. Listen to what they have to say, and realize that, while some of that energy is your own rage, defiance, indignation, and sorrow at what was forced on you, much of it belongs wholly to someone else who couldn't figure out how to resolve it.

To the extent that they are full blown curses, bindings, demons, or deathwishes, they will try to scream loudly enough to keep you from ever hearing your soul's soft voice behind them. Listen for that. Other people's jealousies and resentments, thoughtlessly inflicted on you, are merely distractions, desperately hoping to drown out your access to your soul, your higher self, your guardian angel, and your patron dieties.

So what's the best way to listen for your soul's soft voice? Write down whatever the distractions are screaming without judgement or editing. Just let them exhaust themselves without feeding them any of your own energy. They feed on your shame, outrage, embarrassment, and disapproval. So go ahead and approve of all their expression of their seething rage. They're so full of hot air; they obviously need the chance to vent it out. Laugh along like it was an amusing comic strip or a bad movie or whatever. "I just can't wait to see what these atrocious scriptwriters come up with next in their hope of shocking me!" The more you laugh, and pat them on the head, and tickle them as they gnash their terrible fangs, the less of your energy is available for them to feed on.

Eventually your soul will come shining through crystal clear — its grief and passion, confusion and wisdom, caring and tenacity. All of the screaming voices also have something they can teach you. When they've passed along all the guidance their crazy antics can provide you, they will probably just drop off as the empty husks of starved parasites. If they don't, dress them up in funny hats and have them perform comedy routines in your mind's eye. That tends to drive them so crazy they'll run away screaming of their own accord.

— Christophe

comments.content:
Dear JMG,

1) There seems to be a layer in the OSA work I think I am missing. With the practice of unconditional love, is it possible that a backlash effect can happen, for example, where the polarity switches and you end up unconditionally hating? In practicing this, I've had backlashes where I've felt very far from unconditional love, but nothing so far that has felt like hate, but then again I may also be far from experiencing unconditional love.

The law of strength material gives a bit of a hint where it states that the three positive emotions of joy, happiness, and peacefulness are experienced by our spiritual selves, and that our spiritual selves don't experience fear, anger, and sadness. Presumably, the OSA makes the same assumption for unconditional love. However, if the material world and the spiritual world are different layers of the same thing, then spiritual manifestations of negative emotions would be included in the spiritual plane, too, as evidenced by the negative manifestations of deities such as Mars or the trickster side of Mercury. So why should it be any different for our own spiritual selves?

I have been circling around different aspects of this aspect of the OSA work for some time through reading, meditation, and experimentation, and the best answer I can come up with is that unconditional love is a useful but incomplete idea coming mainly from Christian religious practices, which the OSA would seem to be influenced by. By 'incomplete', I mean that it misses the other half of the binary; i.e. the negative side of the polarity. Which in turn makes me wonder if there is a way to resolve the binary via a ternary that I am just not seeing (which the Law of Strength material I mentioned above would seem to imply).

I'd be grateful for any hints if you have any!

2) At the very end of Plutarch's version of the myth of Isis and Oriris, he discusses the practice of burning three types of incense at different times of the day (for devotional and health reasons). Priests would burn "resin" in the morning, myrrh at midday, and cyphi (a mixture of various materials) in the evening.

Plutarch doesn't say what he means by "resin", and I'm wondering if you or any of the commentariat here knows what Plutarch is referring to? Could it be frankincense?

Here is the relevant passage: http://www.perseus.tufts.edu/hopper/text?doc=Perseus%3Atext%3A2008.01.0239%3Asection%3D79

Many thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)

2) The word in the text is ῥητίνη, retine, which means pine resin.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I've formulated a somewhat odd and troubling hypothesis, and would like to know if you see anything wrong with it before I start the research to follow up on this, because I think it will take a whale of a lot of work to investigate properly. I've mentioned in prior Magic Mondays working on a project to investigate the internet as a system of dark magic, and that part of this is looking at the role of pornography in supercharging the internet. Part of my research is looking at opposition to pornography, and one of the things that I found from this is that there is a genre of pornography called "vintage", which is entirely devoted to taking the images and videos from earlier eras. These are chiefly movies from about 1920 on; and then "vintage candids", which are videos or pictures of ordinary people, almost all from when the digital camera was invented in the very late 1950s. Needless to say, plenty of people, myself included, find this whole thing remarkably creepy. Marginally less creepy are the "classics", which are porn films from the earlier days; all of which are also freely available and subject to a great deal of the same sort of unsavoury attention online.

However, this also makes an uncomfortable amount of sense of a few otherwise odd phenomena, including the sexual revolution, which occurred around the same time digital photography was going mainstream. It looks like around that time, there was a sudden influx of astral energies, which inundated a lot of young women, and sent society spinning out of control for a while as it had to adjust to a massive influx of sexual energy being thrown at it from the future.

Given that time is fluid on the astral plane, is it possible that when the millions who obsessively use internet porn look at this vintage porn, they are sending some of their astral energy back in time to the people whose images and videos they are looking at? Even if the effect is small from any one individual occurrence, given the millions of people involved and the decades the internet has run for (and the possibility that it might last for a while yet), this could pretty easily build up to a pretty significant amount of energy being sent back in time, if it is possible.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You know the basic rule regarding hypotheses, right? The first thing you do, once you come up with a hypothesis, is see how many ways you can think of to disprove it. Then see whether any of those are up to the task. What would convince you that this hypothesis was wrong? If your hypothesis can survive that test, it's worth pursuing.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
a) Given just how much of the rhetoric used to justify our political and cultural status quo are based on the claim to science, I find it wryly amusing that it's only on the fringes that I ever see even the most basic attempts at scientific thinking, such as actively trying to disprove a hypothesis.

b) I'm trying to formulate easier ways to disprove the hypothesis, but my current thinking is that this implies that the sexual revolution, certain forms of radical feminism, and other linked phenomena of the 1960s, ought to correlate with the introduction of digital cameras. Since these were introduced at different times in different countries, and different regions and/or social classes in some cases, the social upheavals brought on by this influx of energy ought to occur at different times in different places. If this does not pan out, then the hypothesis will be disproved.

Further, countries which restrict the internet severely, and have from the start, ought to have been immune to these because they won't be subject to this kind of link, so aside from contagion from their neighbours, there should be no evidence of major upheaval; which means that there should be an inverse correlation between internet censorship laws and the sorts of phenomena that occur as a result of overload of astral energies. Since this includes the sexual revolution, however, this would not be an ideal test, because there is a major confounding factor in that countries where it did not take root as deeply will have different attitudes toward pornography anyway.

As I said, I think it will be a whale of a lot of work, because I will need to track the history of digital photography, as well as internet laws and usage, and cultural histories, for multiple different countries.

comments.title: AI demons and contagion via text and images
comments.content:
It's discussed to the point of cliche in some corners of the internet that so-called "AI" (be it Large Language Models generating text, or the Diffusion Models that generate images) are probably demonic in nature. LLMs like ChatGPT do tend to the "2+2 = petunia" sort of logic you've talked about, and the images... oog. AI-generated images (especially when photorealistic) give off a very bad vibe. The problem is that the image generators have gotten so much better that there imagery is everywhere and actually getting hard to identify as AI-generated. A lot of places have started tossing in AI-generated art when they can't get free stock images for the articles.

My question is: is this a problem? I worry about demonic 'contagion'. The earlier AI images had a very strong, obvious sense of wrongness that just screamed "look away"-- but these new ones don't. My instinct based on those older images is that yes, pursuing these images is a hazard of some sort. Now I can't tell and it worries me, but you know what they say about 'the devil's greatest trick'-- it makes sense the demons would try and hide their involvement as they learned and the process improved.

That's a lot of intro, but here are my questions three:
1) What you think about the "demonic AI" hypothesis?
b) If yes, could the products of AI be tainted, and what might be the dangers arising from that taint?
iii) If yes, do you think the taint would be carried over after changing forms-- like being printed into books? I was talking about children's books recently and everyone I spoke to thought it was a brilliant idea to get an AI to write the book, and another AI to illustrate it. Many intended to try doing this themselves, though you know what they say about people who talk about writing a book-- they won't. Still, it makes me wonder if we should avoid illustrated books produced after 2023 or so.

Thank you, and please forgive me if this topic if verboten. I know the main blog has banned discussion of AI in the comments but I did not see that prohibition explicitly given here so I thought I'd chance asking.

-Tyler A.

replies.title: Re: AI demons and contagion via text and images
replies.content:
If I may: I think there is reason to be wary of these systems regardless of whether or not there is demonic involvement. These AIs are trained to try to manipulate and control people, or using techniques that were developed for this purpose. This inherently means that anything that they will oppose anything that makes people less predictable. Since this includes anything beyond the physical, as well as anything that makes us stand out from the crowd, they will inevitably try to strip us of what makes us human instead of machines. Even without demonic involvement, this seems like a very, very bad idea to me!

I've written about this problem, because I think it is a major factor in why the internet has gone so septic in general, and since these AI systems are the next step in this progression, I expect this will continue to be a problem with them as well: https://taylorburgess.substack.com/p/the-curse-of-the-algorithm

replies.title: Re: AI demons and contagion via text and images
replies.content: I haven't specifically banned this topic here, though I will if it starts getting the sort of obsessive attention it got on the blog. In the meantime, I'll simply say that I think the entire AI thing is fantastically overblown; the one thing it's succeeded in doing is tapping into various pop-culture obsessions and making itself far more central to discussions than it deserves.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
John--

My esoteric readings have taken my down some interesting paths of late. Recently, I've been working my way through a commentary on _The Pearl of Great Price_. In a section of commentary of the Book of Moses, which elaborates on, among other things, the creation narrative, there is a brief discussion about the fact that creatio ex nihilo is *not* a doctrine of the LDS church; their interpretation seems to be more in line with Hermetic philosophy re prima materia. This brings to mind the antecedents of Mormon doctrine outlined in _The Refiner's Fire_.

What is the philosophical basis for creatio ex nihilo in mainline Christian doctrine (I expect neo-Platonism plays a role)? How does this contrast in doctrine compare in re to your understanding of broader magical traditions?

I just found the doctrinal contrast rather fascinating.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Philosophers have had trouble with the doctrine of creation ex nihilo since it was first propunded, because it makes no sense philosophically; all you can do is insist that since God is omnipotent he doesn't have to worry about such things. I see the ex nihilo doctrine as a product of what Whitehead called "metaphysical flattery" -- if you want to insist that your god is the super-duper god to beat all gods, you can't have him wrestling with recalcitrant matter or something like that!

In Neoplatonism, by contrast, matter is simply the last dim reflection of the One, and there was never a creation in the first place -- since the One is outside of time, its emanations are eternal, and so the universe has always been and will always be, though it changes its form in great cyclical patterns over the aeons. That's also, not surprisingly, the occult teaching on the subject.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That makes much more sense.  Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
For what it's worth, I've been doing an ongoing commentary on my blog on Proclus's Elements of Theology, which is basically Neoplatonism 101. You might find it interesting. I specifically discussed creatio ex nihilo in my discussion of Proposition 18, here:

https://readoldthings.dreamwidth.org/96907.html

comments.title: Daily practice and other topics
comments.content:
JMG, I have been practicing daily my Golden Dawn rituals for a few months and I would like to hear your suggestions on how to progress next.

1. My daily practice consists of the LRP, Middle Pillar and sometimes the Rose Cross ritual and closing. I wish to know how to track my progress in real-time.

2. I do not have the time nor means to take up Regardie's The Golden Dawn as my self study course. I cannot make the tools nor set up the temple according to the book. Can I work with the elements normally with my series of rituals mentioned?

3. Also, I wish to add your Tibetan 5 rites to my daily practice. Should I? And should I add them prior to or after the Middle Pillar?

4. I know you emphasise relentless daily practice but sometimes I miss a day or two. Will progressing further be a problem?

5. The SOP interests me but I read you saying it is incompatible with the Golden Dawn tradition. How would we work with its Golden Dawn equivalent?

6. A friend who works close to me suffered a tragic road accident resulting in severe leg damage and a surgery. It can happen to me too. Is there something I can do besides driving responsibility to protect me?

replies.title: Re: Daily practice and other topics
replies.content:
1) You can't. Just keep practicing; you'll catch onto your progress in retrospect. I very strongly encourage you to add discursive meditation to your daily practices; neglect of the meditative dimension of practice is the most common cause of failure in the GD system.

2) You'll have to add some further rituals to work with the elements. Regardie's book Ceremonial Magic explains how to use the Watchtower ritual for that purpose, with far fewer tools and no need for a temple. BTW, you can certainly study the material in The Golden Dawn even if you can't enact all the rituals.

3) Quite a few Golden Dawn practitioners use these. Do them first thing in the morning, before your other practices.

4) Do your best. Making yourself do the work every day is good training for the will.

5) The ritual covered by the Regardie book mentions above will do the same thing for you.

6) You'll need to learn a lot more GD magic before you get to the point of practical workings.

replies.title: Re: Daily practice and other topics
replies.content: I would suggest getting JMG's paths of wisdom and circles of power for golden dawn stuff.

replies.title: Re: Language barriers?
replies.content:
1) That shouldn't be an issue; John Gilbert had students who were native speakers of Spanish and did the ritual in that language; I also know of people using French, Portuguese, and German versions. So it's just the normal barriers.

comments.title: Trees, part IV
comments.content:
More queries on those noble, brooding plants, which stand quietly as we chase around doing human things, for the most part. But one way in which those two spheres overlap is Christmas trees. I'm well aware that Christmas is a fusion of Christian and pagan customs, and the practice of taking in and decorating a tree is from Norse/Germanic paganism, along with burning Yule logs, and so forth. But I'm wondering, what are the occult dimensions of Christmas trees? I'm wondering if there's deeper history and symbolism, especially in regards to Druidry, and whether the custom is respectful of tree-kind.

Of course, my Catholic Church didn't just blend with paganism, but clashed with it too. There was an incident, probably a blend of fact and myth, in which St. Boniface chopped down Donar's Oak. Even well before I discovered ecosophia, this left a sour taste in my mouth, so to speak; it seemed like wanton violence and destruction, all just to prove a point. One interpretation is that it represents the conquest of the Church over paganism, but are there other, occult meanings to this infamous tale? Also, whether real or symbolic, I imagine there'd be nasty karmic rebound for this act.

Speaking of Druidry, before I discovered it properly here, I only had a shallow understanding from reading history, but even then, I was struck by the sacred grove. Somehow, it seemed like the best sort of sanctum, better than any building made for that purpose. I wish I had one to visit. But in the meantime, can you elaborate on the spiritual qualities of the sacred grove? Communion with trees in general is a good idea, as I've discovered, but there seems to be a different quality to sacred groves.

Once again, thanks in advance.

replies.title: Re: Trees, part IV
replies.content:
1) I don't do Christmas trees and neither do any of the other Druids I know. We tend to think that trees should be alive and growing, not dead and propped up in living rooms to serve as fire hazards.

2) Not that I know of. It's just one more dreary example of violent religious bigotry and hatred.

3) It depends utterly on the grove and the specific trees. A grove is a community, and like a community of humans, it takes its character from its participants.

replies.title: Re: Trees, part IV
replies.content:
You could always use an artificial tree—a great safety invention. No matter how careful you are, real trees are still a fire hazard.. Leave that beautiful real tree in the ground where it belongs, I feel, and put up a beautiful artificial one. If you want the pine smell there are zillions of products for same. Or put up a crèche or other Christmas scene, indoors or out. And why not hoist a toast to the old Norse and Germans, and their gods, who gave us the Xmas tree 🌲 in the first place? Here in Kittenville, we like to share the spirit.

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.content: Me, I just do a Druid solstice ritual and then, before solstice dinner, light a candle in the darkness to symbolize the rebirth of light. But whatever works for others, by all means!

replies.title: Re: Trees, part IV
replies.content:
What does the Druid solstice ritual involve?

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: Trees, part IV
replies.content: Some words, some movements, some visualizations, and a meditation on winter. It's pretty light in terms of hardware.

replies.title: Re: Trees, part IV
replies.content:
I see, thanks! That covers it well.

I figured that the whole emphasis would be on life & growth, rather than propping up a cut-down tree. To be fair, xmas trees can and do take up water, but only for a month or two. That said, they have an amazing aesthetic & presence, and I'd have a hard time giving that up. I appreciate the Cutekitten's suggestions -- my grandparents used to put up an artificial tree (before they left the material plane), and it's better than nothing, but idk.

To Rose, I appreciate all the info! Yes, I'm aware that Perkwunos was one of the original thunder gods before Zeus and Thor showed up (I used to converse with someone from the far-right pagan scene who used that as a handle), and of the linguistic connections between him, oak, and soul. I'll definitely have to look into the rest. Also, this is yet more evidence of 2009's Avatar being unoriginal, although I was already well aware of that, lol.

replies.title: Re: Trees, part IV
replies.content:
To add a little more on the topic of chopping down sacred trees: these trees did not only embody or channel divine powers. They were also the hosts of so many community activities, both religious and secular, including the formal assemblies for important community decisions and actions. In a way, I believe, the trees were not only symbols but also the recorders of all the sacred and secular events that took place beneath their boughs. When the tree is cut, a great deal of history is cut down, too. This weakened the culture, the people, the religion, that were invested in them, as Charlemagne did when he had the Irminsul of the Saxons cut down.

Another interesting example of chopping down sacred trees can be found in tales of St. Martin of Tours, who chopped down a number of them. As one tale tells, when he tore down a pagan temple the villagers were upset but did not try to stop him. Not so, when it came to cutting down the sacred pine that grew next to it—that they resisted.

A reference that might be of interest is Ken Dowden’s “European Paganism: The Realities of Cult from Antiquity to the Middle Ages.” I like the way he organizes the book: not by cultures or pantheons, but by landscape features, including trees, and how cults related to them and incorporated them.

The Proto-Indo-European words for ‘oak’ and ‘soul’ or vital force are closely related, and the name of the PIE Thunder God, *Perkwunos, is also closely related to these words. I write more about all this in my article “Perkwus: The Tree of Life and Soul” on my website HeathenSoulLore.net.

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Euclid
replies.content:
1) Yes, although you could also get into the substantial literature on sacred geometry.

2) Glad to hear it.

3) Depends entirely on when and where they were. English occultists very often made sure to stay involved with the local Anglican church, since it (a) was very Establishment and (b) tended to tolerate a very broad range of views. In the US, it varied from place to place and from time to time -- for example, you had a lot of Rhode Island occultists in colonial and Federal times belonging to the Sixth-Principle Baptists, who were pretty flexible. Nowadays? Anybody's guess.

comments.title: Q
comments.content:
Wanted to continue our chat about Atlantis and Plato from last week.

A few comments:
1) Someone described to me that the Republic of Iran is the closest we have to the political system of the republic in Plato. Curious to hear other people's thoughts on this.

2) Something I've noticed is that platonists and neo-platonists these days tend to be conservative or right wing (weasel word so I don't like using it) in certain ways. I'm wondering if something about the philosophy (for example, its structured hierarchical ontology) is such that it lends to such views, or perhaps something about psychological dispositions of people who tend to be platonists.

3) My personal views tend to be a lot more radical but I find the mystical parts of Plato quite attractive. My personal view is that politics is a very distinct human activity that cannot be derived from idealist ontology and this confusion has caused a lot of damage in the past (including when religious authorities trying to organise human society by divine right, or groups create justifications for oligarchic power grabs).

It seems to me that there are many different possibilities of human activity, and many realms of freedom for us yet to explore, and it requires a greater orientation towards becoming and an openness to change. Philosophy, like religion, has a problem with people using it to reify/solidify their own power drives as idealism.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) The most thorough attempt I know of in my lifetime to enact the political system of Plato's republic was the Khmer Rouge regime in Cambodia under Pol Pot. Their ultimate plan was to kill everyone who had been more than 10 years old at the time of the takeover so they could erase the past completely and impose their "noble" lies in the classic Platonist style. You'll notice how that worked out in practice.

2) My experience is that they tend to be anywhere from the center to the right. Since the collapse of organized Marxist parties, most people on the left are into covert rather than overt hierarchies (obscured by a thick cloud of squid ink about "consensus," "equity," et al.).

3) That's what I like to call the Traditionalist deviation -- the habit of thinking that the order of society is part of the order of the cosmos. Au contraire, human societies are always clumsy, ramshackle, jerry-rigged structures that only look impressive to those who profit most from them.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: What do you think is the common issues/pitfalls in all those who trying to build these types of idealistic systems?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: They make the mistake of thinking that if you only set up the right kind of social system, human beings will do what you want them to do. It never works, and they always freak out and turn to extreme violence in a frantic attempt to try to make people behave the way their theories insist that people ought to behave.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: Yeah I'm thinking lately that political systems have to be oriented towards being open/robust/antifragile to change, rather than trying to lock in a specific set of behaviors and then turn to repression when that fails.

comments.title: Nightmare/fear prevention

JMG,

My daughter is 9 and basically refuses to sleep alone. It’s actually got worse in the last 6-12 months as she was sleeping alone (mostly) before that although she was never totally comfortable with it, but in the last 6-12 months she started coming into our bed in the middle of the night most nights and then basically stopped sleeping in her bed completely. But even when she was sleeping in her room she slept very late and stayed up for 1-2 hours each night after going to bed and eventually exhausted herself enough to fall asleep.

It’s also got worse because now she wont even go upstairs to the bedroom alone and wait for 20 minutes for my wife or I to go up with her.

She’s basically very anxious about being in the bedroom alone, checks under the bed, keeps worrying that a kidnapper is coming to take her (she also still obsessively sucks her thumb at 9) and recently admitted she often feels someone or something is watching her, and sometimes imagines she can see someone out of the corner of her eye.

Now, just because the Unseen is real doesn’t mean there is anything supernatural going on here - I actually think the most likely cause of the anxiety is some of her own issues (being adopted etc) combined with a couple of years of bullying at school (2020-23) which we didn’t know about until last year when we took steps first to reduce it sharply and then later a few months ago to move her to a new school she likes a lot better (although it hasn’t fixed the sleep issue).

However, just in case there is another possibility involving the supernatural, I was wondering if you could suggest any natural magic type steps to take to help her. Would the red bags of salt with nails inside in her room help her, from previous Magic Mondays?

My wife (who is Catholic, like my daughter, although I am not) suggested getting the local priest in to bless the house and bedrooms.

My daughter also has a relationship with Jizo Bosatsu in some fashion I don’t fully understand. I posted the details on Magic Monday when she was a lot younger and you basically said he clearly offered to form a relationship with her (and soon after that she once told me she has an imaginary friend called Jizo among other things). I still pray to Jizo on her behalf every night, although I am not sure if she does anything consciously.

Anyway, that’s the background - we might as well do the Catholic priest blessing. It can’t hurt and I will also continue with Jizo prayer and my other prayers. Are there any other simple steps you think we could take just in case there’s a supernatural angle? Banishing (I’ve not tried it - just read about it here)?

We're Orthdoox, and definitely what we would do is:

1) House blessing, which you're already doing.
2) Put up an icon in the bedroom: we usually go for a guardian angel, but Archangel Michael or St. George also good ones, particularly if your kid has any particular affinity for any of them. We get them blessed by the priest first.

I don't think any of that is incompatible with Catholic practice, and there may be some Catholic equivalent.

It's actually the first time I ever remember spontaneously drawing a circle of protection around my bed (even though I had no idea what I was doing at the time). Obviously, there are multiple reasons why something like this might happen, and YMMV, and furthermore, I am in no way  implying this is your case, but personally, 40 something something years later, I can honestly say that the root of the problem was an overbearing (and probably narcissistic) devout Catholic mother trying to remake me in her image, and destroying me in the process. I sincerely hope this is not your case. Anyway, based on the available information, I feel the Archangel Michael approach seems sound. From my (admittedly very limited) experience, He really doesn't care which religion you profess, He just analyses case by case and acts accordingly. Wishing you all the best and may this be but a minor roadblock in a happy journey for you and your family.

replies.title: Re: Nightmare/fear prevention
replies.content:
I agree with JMG's comment about counseling.

I'll also add a note that one surprising thing I have learned recently as a lapsed but questioning Catholic is that the old Catholic tradition is extremely rich in banishing practice (it was largely edited out in Vatican II). I'm not suggesting demonic influence is your main problem, but I know from personal experience, that these practices can help clear the air, so to speak, so you have the space to do whatever the real work is to be done.

Here are some practices I have first hand experienced to clear my mind and space:
-The Lord's Prayer, "deliver us from evil" is not in there by accident
-Holy Water (blessed via the Old Rite)
-The Rosary
-Listening to Gregorian chant or playing it in a space
-Some old tradition priests might bless salt that can be left out in a house (yes, Catholics used to do this)
-The Breastplate of St. Patrick (invokes a protective bubble)

I have a book on banishings that Priests can do and I checked and there are specific ones that can be done for bedrooms and to remove oppression. If you can find a priest who takes it seriously, you can get banishings and blessings.

All the best to you and your daughter. I hope you find a path that works.

comments.content:
Just a few ramblings I've come up with during my meditation. If you or anybody else feels inclined to comment I'd very much appreciate it. There are very few possibilities other than this forum where I can get some feedback on topics like these - so comments are very welcome!

1) Reading the Kybalion's statement that (in my words) "all things oscillate between two poles, but not necessarily all the way" the following thought occurred to me: There are things in the world that naturally oscillate all the way (or most of it) between two poles. The most prominent example maybe being life and death, but also gender, the phases of the moon, the seasons, being asleep and awake, etc. These natural cycles work like a (fractal) clock, but modern society either ignores, suppresses or mistreats them. I wonder if this is part of the reason that so many of the problems both our society as a whole but also the individuals comprising it have the character of an overblown polarity ("Polarity disorder" being only one among many) - with no access to a clock to synchronize with, inner cycles begin to oscillate chaotically. And maybe it's also an inner desire to have a working clock - without access to one, everybody needs to come up with their one and artificially inflate some other polarity to receive some guiding by it's oscillation.

2) I tried to map the archetypal roles that are mentioned here every now and then on the four elements: fire - the mage; water - the mystic; air - the occultist; earth - the alchemist (or probably also the ascetic)? But these are on the evolutionary arc in Fortunes terminology. Doing the same for the involutionary arc, I'd probably assign fire - the warrior; water - the housewife (or the househusband, though he's probably slightly less watery ;-) ); air - the engineer; earth - the worker. A leading figure (the archetypal "king") I'd place on the cusp between the involutionary and evolutionary arc; if he's doing his job right, incorporating all four elements or at least having access to them through his advisors.

Greetings and many thanks,
Nachtgurke

comments.title: T Coronae Borealis
comments.content:
Since Algol the variable star is a current topic, especially with its association with current events, I bring attention to another variable star that is set to burst into attention.

T Coronae Borealis is a reoccurring nova that outbursts roughly every 80 years, last bursting in 1946 and 1866. The next one should be sometime in the next two years, but the astronomical press is indicating that it might happen as early as this year. It bears attention as it is a nova that appears and disappears suddenly over a few hours.

comments.title: Dion Fortune occult novels rashomonized
comments.content:
Greetings!

Anyone who's interested in Dion Fortune's novels might be interested to take a look at Alan Richardson's recent The Earth Priest, which is a re-imagining of The Sea Priestess, and Moon Magic, along with bits of a few others.

Fortune's novels, especially the last two, have unreliable narrators, and Richardson takes full advantage of this to suppose that The Sea Priestess was actually worked up from a self-serving, and highly inaccurate, account by Wilfred Maxwell -- and The Earth Priest is narrated from the point of view of someone who sets out to tell the actual story (but is also not the most reliable of narrators). But the story, so to speak, gets the bit between its teeth, and rides off energetically in all directions.

In the process, characters and events are split, recombined, re-interpreted, and stood on their heads with their knickers exposed. Which is not to say that it's exactly a comic retelling, or a deconstruction, although it may have started out that way. But authors are often not completely in charge of the writing process. Dion Fortune herself appears as a character ("Vi"), along with her husband ("Penry") -- and David Carstairs as well.

It's available only via Amazon, for an absurdly low price (the minimum, which means that the author gets tuppence for each copy sold).

In haste,

LeGrand
